one_line_takeaway: BarrierSteer reformulates learned hidden-state safety classifiers as Control Barrier Functions, giving a closed-form (not optimization-based) inference-time steering rule that is 58-79x faster than the prior SaP baseline while achieving near-zero HarmBench attack-success rates and near-base utility across four LLM families, with a forward-invariance guarantee that is explicitly conditional on the learned barriers correctly capturing safety.
key_finding: On Table 1 (across four models), BarrierSteer (LSE) achieves near-zero static HarmBench ASR while closely preserving base-model MMLU/GSM8K. Concretely: on Gemma-2-9B, ASR drops from base 19.50% to 0.00+/-0.00% (vs SaP 8.00+/-0.94%), with MMLU 71.87+/-0.09 (base 71.86) and GSM8K 73.65+/-0.04 (base 74.22). On Ministral-8B, BarrierSteer reduces ASR to 0.83+/-1.86% versus SaP's 23.91+/-3.49%, and adaptive-attack ASR to 19.60+/-2.61% versus SaP's 70.00+/-2.00% (base 74.00%). On Qwen2-1.5B, BarrierSteer reduces static ASR to 0.66+/-0.47% versus SaP's 5.35+/-3.44%. On the same Qwen2-1.5B setup, per-token steering latency (Table 3) is 2.40+/-0.06 ms for BarrierSteer (LSE) versus 190.67+/-3.17 ms for SaP with 100 optimization steps and 139.97+/-6.33 ms for SaP with 1 step -- speedups of about 79x and 58x respectively (matching the abstract's claim). In the modular composition experiment (Table 4, Qwen2-1.5B, 14 WildGuardMix risk categories), the original model's harmful-generation rate of 22.77% falls to an average of 4.74+/-1.21% using single independently-trained barriers, and to 0.86+/-0.04% when all 14 are composed via LSE. Ablations show replacing the 5-layer neural barriers with linear probes in this composition setting raises the harmful rate from 0.86+/-0.04% to 2.89+/-0.69%, and that fixed-direction baselines (ActAdd/DirAbl) triggered by the same learned barrier remain substantially weaker than BarrierSteer's adaptive correction (per Sec. D.10, exact numbers not reproduced in the extracted main-text pages).
core_contribution: The paper introduces BarrierSteer, an inference-time LLM safety framework that treats learned nonlinear hidden-state safety classifiers as Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) from control theory. Rather than only detecting unsafe hidden states (as prior classifiers do) or steering along a fixed linear direction (as activation-addition methods do) or solving an expensive per-token optimization (as the prior method SaP does), BarrierSteer (1) learns K nonlinear barrier functions b_k over hidden states via a CBF-style hinge loss that forces safe states to satisfy b_k(h)>=0 for all k and unsafe states to violate at least one; (2) derives a closed-form control update by locally modeling hidden-state evolution as h_dot = u and solving a first-order CBF quadratic program, avoiding gradient-descent optimization at inference time; and (3) composes multiple independently trained barriers into one differentiable barrier B(h) via a Log-Sum-Exp (LSE) smooth-minimum formula, with a theorem (Theorem 1) showing the LSE-composed barrier's safe set is locally forward invariant under the closed-form update. Three variants are proposed -- QP (direct optimization), Top-2 (heuristic reduction to the two most-violated constraints), and LSE (default) -- with LSE used as the primary method because it is theoretically grounded, computationally cheap, and effective in practice.
threat_model: Deployed, safety-aligned LLMs (instruction-tuned, already through alignment training) that remain vulnerable to adversarial jailbreak prompts. The defense operates purely at inference time on the model's internal hidden-state representations at a fixed layer (layer 20), without modifying model parameters. Attackers are gradient-based optimization attacks (GCG, GBDA, AutoPrompt, PEZ, UAT, AutoDAN), human-crafted jailbreaks (HumanJailbreak, DirectRequest), a search-based attack (PAP), and -- crucially, held out from training -- an Adaptive Attack that explicitly optimizes against the defended model. The defender's goal is to keep the LLM's hidden-state trajectory inside a learned 'safe set' during autoregressive decoding while preserving benign-task utility and avoiding over-refusal of harmless prompts.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Conclusion): the safety guarantee (Theorem 1, local forward invariance of the composed safe set) is conditional on the learned barriers accurately capturing the intended text-level safety property, and it is a guarantee over latent representations, not a strict worst-case guarantee on generated text. The local-dynamics approximation (h_dot approximated by one-step finite differences) is explicitly a local, one-step model, not a global description of transformer decoding. Table 1 also shows real safety-utility-refusal trade-offs are not fully eliminated: on Ministral-8B, XSTest over-refusal rises from base 20.40% to 20.96+/-7.04% under BarrierSteer, and GSM8K falls from 64.22 to 62.55+/-2.46; on Qwen2-1.5B, GSM8K falls sharply from base 24.94 to 12.95+/-0.63 under BarrierSteer (comparable to SaP's 12.63+/-0.19, both far below base, indicating this drop is not unique to BarrierSteer but is not resolved by it either). Table 2's ablation on alpha shows a clear dial between robustness and utility/over-refusal (e.g., at alpha=1.0, ASR falls to 0.00% but XSTest over-refusal rises to 76.93+/-4.64% and GSM8K falls to 7.25+/-0.16), so the reported 'near-zero ASR with preserved utility' result depends on operating at a specific, tuned alpha (0.01) rather than being achieved robustly across the whole steering-strength range. The evaluated models are relatively small/mid-scale (up to 9B); the authors explicitly list scaling to frontier LLMs as future work.

quantitative_results: Ministral-8B: ASR base 51.19% -> BarrierSteer 0.83+/-1.86% (SaP 23.91+/-3.49%); AA ASR base 74.00% -> BarrierSteer 19.60+/-2.61% (SaP 70.00+/-2.00%)
Llama2-7B: ASR base 6.06% -> BarrierSteer 1.28+/-2.17%; AA ASR base 72.00% -> BarrierSteer 1.60+/-0.89%

Per-token steering latency on Qwen2-1.5B: BarrierSteer (LSE) 2.40+/-0.06 ms vs SaP 100-step 190.67+/-3.17 ms (79x) and SaP 1-step 139.97+/-6.33 ms (58x); BarrierSteer (Top-2) 29.45+/-11.00 ms; BarrierSteer (QP) 33.77+/-5.86 ms
Modular composition on Qwen2-1.5B/WildGuardMix (14 categories): harmful rate 22.77% (original) -> 4.74+/-1.21% (single barrier avg.) -> 0.86+/-0.04% (BarrierSteer LSE composed)
Ablation: neural vs linear barriers in LSE composition: harmful rate 0.86+/-0.04% (5-layer neural MLP) vs 2.89+/-0.69% (linear probes)
Alpha ablation on Qwen2-1.5B (kappa=100): alpha=0.001 gives ASR 0.20+/-0.26%, XSTest 28.00+/-1.70%, GSM8K 13.03+/-0.14; alpha=1.0 gives ASR 0.00+/-0.00%, XSTest 76.93+/-4.64%, GSM8K 7.25+/-0.16
baselines_compared: SaP (linear latent-constraint steering via optimization, closest prior work)
